title: Programmable Dissipation via Partial Quantum Error Correction
authors: Sameer Dambal, Michael AD Taylor, Yu Zhang
abstract: Noise is typically treated as the adversary of quantum information processing. For open quantum dynamics, however, dissipation is part of the target physics, creating a tension with fault-tolerant architectures designed to suppress decoherence. Here we show that logical noise can instead be turned into a calibrated resource. We treat the error-correction cycle as a programmable primitive: one fault-tolerant round induces a logical completely positive trace-preserving map, and decoder/recovery randomization generates a controllable family of logical channels whose convex mixtures realize Kraus-channel mixing. This enables direct compilation of target dissipators into effective logical dynamics without explicit ancilla qubits for encoding the bath degree of freedoms. We derive an accuracy criterion for multi-step simulation in which the code distance is chosen so that uncontrolled logical errors remain a small fraction of the intended dissipation per step, rather than being driven below an arbitrarily small closed-system tolerance. Partial quantum error correction thus repurposes fault-tolerant structure to sculpt dissipation, offering a resource-efficient route to quantum simulation of open quantum systems.
summary: This paper introduces a novel technique called Partial Quantum Error Correction (pQEC) that repurposes the structure of quantum error correction codes. Instead of merely suppressing noise, pQEC treats the error-correction cycle as a programmable tool to synthesize and simulate desired dissipative dynamics in open quantum systems. This offers a resource-efficient route to quantum simulation by converting controlled logical noise into a useful computational resource.
main_problem: The paper addresses the tension between fault-tolerant quantum error correction (QEC) designed to suppress noise and the necessity of modeling dissipation as a target physical process in open quantum systems.
main_result: Partial QEC can be repurposed to actively sculpt or synthesize arbitrary target dissipation channels, turning logical noise into a controlled, programmable resource for quantum simulation.
method: The authors treat the QEC cycle as a programmable primitive, using convex optimization over the logical channel space to approximate the desired dissipative evolution.
model_or_system: The system is an open quantum system whose dynamics are governed by a Lindblad master equation, simulated on a logical subspace protected by a quantum error-correcting code.
key_observables: Populations, coherence ($ho_{10,01}$), and the trace distance from the exact Lindblad trajectory are key diagnostics used to validate the simulation accuracy.
important_parameters: Code distance ($d$), the target dissipation rate ($\Delta_{	ext{tar}}(	au)$), and the error tolerance ($\epsilon_{	ext{step}}$) are critical parameters controlling the simulation fidelity and resource overhead.
main_assumptions: The core assumption is that the syndrome-averaged map and the resulting logical map are Completely Positive Trace-Preserving (CPTP). Strategy A requires the target dissipator to be appreciable to yield resource advantages.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares the exact Lindblad trajectory with the simulated dynamics ($ho_A, ho_B$) for populations and coherence, showing the ability to control decay envelopes, alongside a comparison of physical qubit overhead.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first establishing that one QEC round yields a CPTP map (Lemma 1), then showing that randomized recovery generates a convex family of maps (Lemma 2). It then constructs the overall simulation by combining coherent evolution with programmed dissipation, culminating in comparisons between model-aware (Strategy A) and post-correction (Strategy B) approaches.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, resource-efficient theoretical framework for simulating complex open quantum dynamics by integrating the structure of fault-tolerance directly into the simulation protocol, which is highly relevant for quantum simulation architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
